findings.title: SSO/SAML Integration Dependencies
findings.description: The implementation of SSO/SAML integration for enterprise onboarding is dependent on third-party identity providers (Okta, Azure AD, OneLogin). These integrations require thorough testing and validation to ensure compatibility with IdP-initiated and SP-initiated flows.
assumptions: Third-party identity providers have stable APIs.
Existing authentication system can be modified to support SSO.

evidence.excerpt: Need to integrate with Okta, Azure AD, and OneLogin.

recommendations: Conduct a feasibility study on integrating with each identity provider.

findings.title: Data Isolation Compliance Risk
findings.description: The current reliance on application-level row filtering for tenant data isolation poses a compliance risk, particularly for SOC 2 Type II requirements. This must be addressed to avoid potential legal and financial repercussions.
assumptions: SOC 2 Type II compliance is mandatory for enterprise customers.

evidence.excerpt: Need to evaluate and implement proper isolation boundaries.

recommendations: Implement database-level separation for tenant data.

findings.title: Implement Role-Based Access Control
findings.description: To meet enterprise customer needs, a robust role-based access control (RBAC) system must be developed. This includes custom role definitions and resource-level access controls, which are critical for security and compliance.
assumptions: Existing user management system can support RBAC.

evidence.excerpt: Enterprise customers need fine-grained RBAC beyond our current model.

recommendations: Design and implement a new RBAC framework.

findings.title: Data Residency Controls Implementation
findings.description: Implementing data residency controls is essential for compliance with GDPR and data sovereignty laws. This requires significant architectural changes to support region-pinned storage.
assumptions: Current infrastructure can support region-pinned storage.

evidence.excerpt: Need to implement region-pinned storage with cross-region replication disabled.

recommendations: Evaluate current infrastructure and plan for necessary changes.

findings.title: White-Label Theming and Custom Domain Support
findings.description: To meet the needs of enterprise prospects, the platform must support white-label theming and custom domains. This will require architectural changes to allow for tenant-specific branding.
assumptions: Current architecture can be adapted for multi-tenant branding.

evidence.excerpt: Three enterprise prospects require white-label branding.

recommendations: Assess the current architecture for adaptability to white-labeling.

summary: The analysis highlights critical dependencies and risks associated with implementing enterprise-level features in the EnterpriseSuite platform. Key areas such as SSO integration, data isolation, and compliance requirements are identified as high-impact and blocking, necessitating immediate attention in the V1 phase. Other enhancements, such as RBAC and white-label theming, are recommended for subsequent phases.
